35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
360	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
361	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
362	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
363	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
364	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
365	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
366	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
367	  closed embedding.
368	
369	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
370	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
371	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
372	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
373	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
374	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
375	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
376	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
377	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
378	
379	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
380	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
381	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
382	
383	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
384	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
385	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
386	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
387	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
388	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
389	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
390	\end{proof}

392	\section{Group-scheme structure}
393	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
394	
395	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
396	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
397	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
398	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
399	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
400	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
401	
402	\begin{theorem}\leanok
403	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
404	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
406	  
407	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
408	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
409	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
410	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
411	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
412	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
413	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
414	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
415	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
416	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
417	  %  topologies  by
418	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
419	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
420	  %   \end{dfn}"
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
423	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
424	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
425	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
426	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
427	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
428	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
429	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
430	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
431	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
432	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
433	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
434	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
435	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
436	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
437	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
438	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
439	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
440	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
441	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
442	  scheme locally of finite type.
443	\end{theorem}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
446	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
447	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
448	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
449	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
450	% records the standard transfer.
451	\begin{proof}
452	  
453	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
454	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
455	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
456	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
457	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
458	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
459	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
460	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
461	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
462	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
463	  in the value category).
464	
465	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
466	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
467	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
468	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
469	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
470	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
471	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
472	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
473	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
474	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
475	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
476	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
477	\end{proof}
478	
479	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
480	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
481	
482	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
483	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
484	
485	\begin{definition}\leanok
486	[The Picard scheme]
487	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
489	  
490	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
491	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
492	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
493	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
494	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
495	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
496	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
497	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
498	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
499	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
500	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
501	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
502	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
503	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
504	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
505	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
506	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
507	\end{definition}
508	
509	\section{Lean encoding}
510	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
511	
512	The Lean target file is the new
513	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
514	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
515	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
518	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
519	
520	\begin{itemize}
521	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
522	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
523	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
524	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
525	    \texttt{representable} below.
526	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
527	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
528	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
529	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
530	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
531	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
532	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
533	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
534	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
535	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
536	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
537	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
538	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
539	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
540	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
541	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
542	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
543	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
544	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
545	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
546	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
547	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
548	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
549	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
550	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
551	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
552	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
553	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
554	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
555	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
556	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
557	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
558	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
559	\end{itemize}
560	
561	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
562	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
563	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
564	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
565	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
566	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
567	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
568	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the
569	gap reported to the plan agent, as it is logically downstream of the
570	Hilbert-scheme construction of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
571	
572	\section{Sorry-by-sorry closure order}
573	\label{sec:fga_pic_sorry_closure_order}
574	
575	The Lean target file
576	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} currently
577	carries seven open sorries, namely one free \texttt{sorry} inside the
578	proof of the smooth-proper quotient lemma and six \texttt{⟨sorry⟩}
579	instance bodies for the six carrier typeclasses
580	\texttt{HasPicSharp}, \texttt{HasDivFunctor}, \texttt{HasPicScheme},
581	\texttt{HasAbelMap}, \texttt{PicSharpRepresentable},
582	\texttt{PicSchemeGroupObject}. The carrier-soundness refactor of the
583	preceding section isolates each typeclass instance as the unique
584	sorry-carrying site for its associated extracted definition, so a
585	consumer that abstracts over the typeclass is kernel-clean. This
586	section is the explicit closure-order plan: for each sorry we record
587	(i) the Lean location and identifier, (ii) the mathematical content
588	to be proved, (iii) the Mathlib prerequisites at the project's pinned
589	revision, (iv) the Route~C (Riemann--Roch) substrate dependency, and
590	(v) a closure-order rank, where rank~1 is an independent carrier that
591	can be discharged from existing Mathlib alone, rank~2 depends on a
592	prior carrier or a Mathlib gap that is not Riemann--Roch, and rank~3
593	depends on a Route~C substrate re-engagement.
594	
595	\medskip
596	
597	The ranks are not a serialisation: rank-1 carriers may be closed in
598	parallel; rank-2 and rank-3 carriers depend either on other carriers
599	in the same chapter, on sibling A.1.c / A.2.b chapters
600	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
601	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), or on the Route~C arc of the project
602	strategy (the Riemann--Roch development for smooth proper curves over
603	a field). The strategic point of the ranks is to identify which
604	sorries can be attempted first while the Route~C arc remains
605	substrate-blocked.

719	\subsection{Sorry 3 --- \texttt{instHasAbelMap} (L294)}
720	\label{subsec:sorry_has_abel_map}
721	
722	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
723	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
724	line~294; instance
725	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasAbelMap}.
726	
727	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts existence of
728	the Abel map
729	$A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ as a natural
730	transformation of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$, sending a relative
731	effective Cartier divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$ to its associated
732	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) = \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$.
733	This is the substantive content of
734	\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
735	
736	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
737	\begin{itemize}
738	  \item Natural transformation \texttt{${\rightsquigarrow}$} of
739	    functors, \texttt{Nonempty}: \emph{exists}.
740	  \item Dual of an ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$ as an invertible
741	    sheaf for a relative effective Cartier divisor: \emph{partial}.
742	    Mathlib has dual modules and dual sheaves but the specific link
743	    "ideal of a relative effective Cartier divisor is invertible and
744	    its dual is the associated line bundle" is a project-internal
745	    bridge.
746	  \item Functoriality of the assignment under pullback (Kleiman \S3
747	    Lem.~\texttt{lm:ctn}): \emph{needs-bridge}. Pullback of relative
748	    effective divisors along base-change is standard for flat-over-
749	    base divisors; Mathlib's coverage of this for the scheme-theoretic
750	    relative setting is incomplete.
751	\end{itemize}
752	
753	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the Abel map is a
754	construction at the level of line bundles and divisors, not a
755	consequence of Riemann--Roch. (Riemann--Roch tells us that the Abel
756	map for a fixed degree-$g$ pole-set is surjective onto $\Pic^g_{C/k}$;
757	this is a downstream consequence, not a prerequisite for defining the
758	map.)
759	
760	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} The component-wise definition
761	of $A_{C/k}(T)$ is: given $D \in \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)$, set
762	$A_{C/k}(T)(D) := [\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}]$ in
763	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$. Naturality follows from the pullback
764	formula for the dual ideal sheaf. The Lean encoding is a sequence of
765	three constructions: (a) extract the ideal sheaf of a relative
766	effective Cartier divisor, (b) dualise to get the line bundle,
767	(c) pass to the \'etale sheafified class. Each step is independent of
768	Riemann--Roch and depends only on Sorries~1 and~2 having committed to
769	a semantic witness for \texttt{picSharp} and \texttt{divFunctor}.
770	
771	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on Sorries~1
772	and~2; no Riemann--Roch substrate).

774	\subsection{Sorry 4 --- \texttt{smoothProperQuotient} body (L354)}
775	\label{subsec:sorry_smooth_proper_quotient}
776	
777	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
778	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
779	line~354; the only free \texttt{sorry} in the file (i.e.\ not inside
780	an instance constructor). It is the proof body of theorem
781	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}, whose
782	conclusion type is \texttt{P.IsRepresentable}.
783	
784	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
785	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
786	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
787	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
788	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
789	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
790	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
791	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
792	
793	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
794	\begin{itemize}
795	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
796	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
797	    \emph{exists}.
798	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
799	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
800	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
801	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
802	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
803	    isolated as a named result.
804	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
805	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
806	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
807	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
808	    not in Mathlib in any form.
809	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
810	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
811	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
812	\end{itemize}
813	
814	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the lemma is a
815	structural fact about quasi-projective schemes and \'etale sheaves,
816	independent of curves and Riemann--Roch. It does, however, sit on the
817	Mathlib gap "effective equivalence relations of schemes", which is a
818	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
819	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the
820	flat-and-proper descent of equivalence relations from EGA IV~17).
821	
822	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:
823	\begin{enumerate}
824	  \item[(a)] Wait for the Mathlib gap "effective equivalence
825	    relations of quasi-projective schemes" to be filled and import
826	    the result. This is the cleanest long-term path but is outside
827	    the project's control timeline.
828	  \item[(b)] Formalise the Altman--Kleiman construction directly in
829	    the project as a sibling Lean file, gated on the EGA IV~8.11.5
830	    lemma (proper monomorphism is a closed immersion) which can be
831	    extracted from existing Mathlib infrastructure in a focused
832	    sub-task.
833	\end{enumerate}
834	Route~(b) is the recommended iter-199+ attempt: the EGA IV~8.11.5
835	extraction is bounded and the remaining structural construction
836	(graph of the equivalence relation inside the Hilbert scheme of $Z$,
837	descent to a closed subscheme of $\Hilb_{Z/k}$) is intrinsic.
838	
839	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (substantial Mathlib
840	gap; no Riemann--Roch substrate; recommended first attempt under the

843	\subsection{Sorry 5 --- \texttt{instHasPicScheme} (L236)}
844	\label{subsec:sorry_has_pic_scheme}
845	
846	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
847	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
848	line~236; instance
849	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasPicScheme}.
850	
851	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts that there
852	exists an object $X \in \mathrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ together with a
853	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness for $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. In
854	other words: the Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ exists. The semantic
855	content is the conclusion of Kleiman \S4 Thm.~\texttt{th:main}
856	specialised by Cor.~\texttt{cor:algsch} to $S = \Spec k$ and $X = C$
857	a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
858	
859	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
860	\begin{itemize}
861	  \item \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Nonempty},
862	    \texttt{Over}: \emph{exists}.
863	  \item \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1},
864	    \texttt{IsProper}, \texttt{GeometricallyIntegral}: \emph{exists}
865	    as typeclasses, but the rich theory needed to actually produce a
866	    representing scheme is largely missing.
867	  \item The FGA representability machinery itself (the four-step
868	    construction of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, namely
869	    Hilbert polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel
870	    map factorisation, smooth-proper quotient): \emph{missing}.
871	  \item Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity / $m$-regularity for
872	    cohomologically twist-vanishing sheaves: \emph{missing}.
873	  \item Riemann--Roch for line bundles on a smooth proper curve over
874	    a field (used in the $m$-regularity step to bound twists by the
875	    degree): \emph{missing} (Route~C).
876	\end{itemize}
877	
878	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} \textbf{Required}. The
879	$m$-regularity bound (Kleiman \S4 Step~2 of the proof of
880	Thm.~\texttt{th:main}) is the point at which the structural argument
881	meets Riemann--Roch: for a curve $C/k$ of genus $g$ and a line
882	bundle $\mathcal{L}$ of degree $d$, $H^1(C, \mathcal{L}(n)) = 0$ for
883	$n \geq m$ depends on $m \geq 2g - 1 - d + \deg \mathcal{O}_C(1)$, which
884	is the Serre vanishing consequence of Riemann--Roch. Specifically the
885	required result is the curve specialisation of
886	Hartshorne~III.5.2 (Serre vanishing) combined with IV.1.3
887	(Riemann--Roch for curves). Without Riemann--Roch, the $m$-regularity
888	step cannot be discharged and the FGA construction does not
889	terminate.
890	
891	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Conditional on the Route~C
892	re-engagement that the project STRATEGY.md flags as
893	substrate-blocked: once the Riemann--Roch arc lands, the $m$-regularity
894	step can be discharged via Hartshorne III.5.2 / IV.1.3, then the
895	remaining three steps (stratification, Abel-map factorisation,
896	smooth-proper quotient via Sorry~4) assemble into the existence
897	witness. Until then this sorry is the load-bearing blocker on the
898	Pic scheme arm of Route~A.
899	
900	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C
901	substrate; load-bearing on the entire A.2.c representability claim).

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}

273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{Algebra.KaehlerDifferential.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler}
342	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
343	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
344	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
345	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
346	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
347	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
348	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
349	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
350	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
351	  % $S'$-modules
352	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
353	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
354	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
355	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
357	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
358	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
359	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
360	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
361	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
362	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
363	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
364	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
365	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
366	  \[
367	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
368	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
371	  \]
372	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
373	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
374	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
375	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
377	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
378	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
379	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
380	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
381	  \[
382	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
383	  \]
384	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
385	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
386	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
387	\end{lemma}
388	
389	\begin{lemma}
390	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
391	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
392	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
393	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
394	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
395	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
396	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
397	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
398	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
399	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
400	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
401	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
402	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
403	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
404	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
405	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
406	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
407	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
408	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
409	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
410	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
411	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
412	  dimension equals \(1\).
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	\begin{proof}
416	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
417	  \begin{enumerate}
418	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
419	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
420	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
421	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
422	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
423	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
424	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
425	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
426	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
427	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
428	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
429	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
430	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
431	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
432	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
433	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
434	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
435	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
436	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
437	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
438	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
439	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
440	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
441	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
442	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
443	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
444	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
445	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
446	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
447	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
448	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
449	      the conclusion.
450	  \end{enumerate}
451	\end{proof}

560	\begin{lemma}
561	  [Smooth over \(\bar k\) \(\Rightarrow\) regular local stalk (Stacks 00TT)]
562	  \label{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth}
564	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT --- Jacobian criterion
565	  % (smooth-implies-regular direction) (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex,
566	  % L38593--38611; tag verified against the Stacks website tag/00TT page,
567	  % "Lemma 10.140.3" in section 10.140 "Smooth algebras over fields").

683	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
684	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every

686	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular

696	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
697	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
698	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).

703	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `` marker on this proof block reflects
704	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
705	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
706	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
707	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
708	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in

710	  % `` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
711	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
712	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper

716	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
717	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
718	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
719	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
720	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
721	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
722	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
723	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
724	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
725	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
726	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
