742	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
743	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
744	
745	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
746	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
747	point.
748	
749	\begin{theorem}
750	
751	\leanok
752	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
753	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
755	  
756	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
757	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
758	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
759	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
760	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
761	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
762	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
763	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
764	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
765	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
766	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
767	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
768	
769	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
770	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
771	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
772	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
773	  in \(X\).
774	\end{theorem}
775	
776	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
777	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
778	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
779	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
780	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
781	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
782	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
783	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
784	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
785	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
786	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
787	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
788	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
789	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
790	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
791	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
792	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
793	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
794	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
795	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
796	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
797	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
798	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
799	% which is a contradiction."
800	\begin{proof}
801	  
802	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
803	
804	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
805	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
806	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
807	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
808	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
809	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
810	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
811	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
812	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
813	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
814	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
815	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
816	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
817	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
818	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
819	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
820	
821	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
822	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
823	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
824	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
825	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
826	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
827	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
828	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
829	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
830	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
831	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
832	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
833	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
834	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
835	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
836	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
837	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
838	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
839	
840	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
841	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
842	  agree on all of \(X\).
843	\end{proof}
844	
845	\begin{remark}
846	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
847	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
848	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
849	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
850	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
851	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
852	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
853	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
854	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
855	
856	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
857	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
858	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
859	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
860	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
861	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
862	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
863	\end{remark}
864	
865	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
866	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
867	
868	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
869	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
870	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
871	this forces the locus to be empty.
872	
873	\begin{lemma}
874	
875	\leanok
876	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
877	  variety]
878	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
879	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
880	  
881	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
882	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
883	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
884	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
885	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
886	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
887	  % divisors).}"
888	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
889	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
890	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
891	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
892	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
893	  union of prime divisors).
894	
895	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
896	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
897	\end{lemma}
898

904	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G

906	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
907	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at

909	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an

914	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
915	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
916	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
917	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)

954	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).

961	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
962	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
963	  identity

966	    \qquad u \in W',

968	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
969	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
970	
971	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
972	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
973	  \(K(X \times X)\):
974	  \[
975	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
976	  \]
977	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
978	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
979	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
980	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
981	
982	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
983	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
984	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
985	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
986	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
987	  \[
988	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
989	  \]
990	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
991	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
992	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
993	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
994	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
995	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
996	  \[
997	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
998	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
999	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1000	  \]
1001	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1002	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1003	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1004	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1005	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1006	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1007	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1008	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1009	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1010	
1011	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1012	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1013	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1014	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1015	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1016	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1017	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1018	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1019	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1020	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1021	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1022	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1023	\end{proof}

1025	\begin{remark}
1026	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1027	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1028	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1029	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1030	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1031	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1032	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1033	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1034	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1035	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1036	\end{remark}

1038	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1039	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1040	
1041	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1042	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1043	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1044	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1045	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1046	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1047	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1048	
1049	\begin{theorem}
1050	
1051	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
1052	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1053

1083	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
1084	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
1085	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
1086	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
1087	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
1088	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
1089	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
1090	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
1091	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
1092	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
1093	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
1094	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
1095	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
1096	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
1097	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
1098	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
1099	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
1100	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
1101	  \begin{enumerate}
1102	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
1103	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
1104	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
1105	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
1106	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
1107	      satisfying
1108	      \[
1109	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
1110	      \]
1111	  \end{enumerate}
1112	
1113	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1114	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
1115	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
1116	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
1117	\end{theorem}
1118	
1119	\begin{proof}
1120	  
1121	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
1122	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
1123	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
1124	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
1125	  \[
1126	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
1127	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1128	  \]
1129	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
1130	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
1131	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
1132	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
1133	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
1134	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
1135	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
1136	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
1137	
1138	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
1139	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1140	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
1141	\end{proof}

1155	\end{remark}

1317	\section{Out of scope}
1318	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1319	
1320	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1321	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1322	scope:
1323	\begin{itemize}
1324	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1325	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1326	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1327	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1328	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1329	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1330	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1331	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1332	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1333	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1334	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1335	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1336	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1337	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1338	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1339	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1340	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1341	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1342	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1343	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1344	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1345	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1346	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1347	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1348	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1349	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1350	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1351	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1352	    introduced.
1353	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1354	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1355	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1356	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1357	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1358	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1359	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1360	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1361	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1362	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1363	\end{itemize}

59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  
102	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
103	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
104	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
105	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
106	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
107	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
108	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
109	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
110	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
111	
112	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
113	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
114	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
115	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
116	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
117	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
118	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
119	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
120	\end{theorem}
121	
122	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
123	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
124	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
125	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
126	then gives the proof.
127	
128	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
129	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
130	
131	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
132	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
133	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
134	scheme).
135	
136	\begin{lemma}\leanok
137	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
146	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
148	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
149	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
155	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
157	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
159	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
161	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
166	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
173	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
174	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on \(C \times J\).
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  
187	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
201	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
204	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
212	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
214	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
216	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
218	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
225	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
226	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}\leanok
230	[Symmetric power of a curve]
231	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
233	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
234	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
235	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
236	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
237	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
238	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
239	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
240	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
241	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
242	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
243	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
244	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
245	  %  having the following properties:
246	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
247	  %        by \(S_r\);
248	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
249	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
250	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
251	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
252	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
253	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
254	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
255	  %  through \(\pi\).
256	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
257	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
258	
259	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
260	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
261	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
262	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
263	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
264	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
265	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
266	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
267	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
268	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
269	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
270	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
271	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
272	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\medskip
276	
277	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised \(g\)-th symmetric power of
278	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
279	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
280	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
281	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq C\)
282	form \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\), then glue via the standard affine
283	patching for an open cover \(C = \bigcup U_i\). This sub-build is a
284	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
285	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
286	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
287	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
288	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
289	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
290	
291	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
292	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
293	
294	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
295	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
296	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
297	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
298	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
299	
300	\begin{lemma}\leanok
301	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
302	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
304	  
305	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
306	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
309	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
310	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
311	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
312	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
313	
314	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
315	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
316	  \[
317	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
318	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
319	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
320	  \]
321	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
322	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
323	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
324	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
325	  \[
326	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
327	  \]
328	  such that
329	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
330	\end{lemma}
331	
332	\begin{proof}
333	  
334	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
335	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
336	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
337	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
338	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
339	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
340	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
341	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
342	  \[
343	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
344	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
345	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
346	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
347	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
348	  \]
349	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
350	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
351	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
352	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
356	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
357	
358	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)
359	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
360	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
361	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
362	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]
366	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
368	  
369	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
370	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "Let \(f^r\) be the map \(C^r\to J\) sending \((P_1,...,P_r)\) to
373	  %  \(f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)\).  On points, \(f^r\) is the map
374	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,
375	  %  and so induces a map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J\).  We can regard
376	  %  \(f^{(r)}\) as being the map sending an effective divisor \(D\) of degree
377	  %  \(r\) on \(C\) to the linear equivalence class of \(D-rP\).  The fibre of
378	  %  the map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)\) containing \(D\) can be
379	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
380	  %  to \(D\), that is, with the linear system \(|D|\).  The image of \(C^{(r)}\)
381	  %  in \(J\) is a closed subvariety \(W^r\) of \(J\), which can also be written
382	  %  \(W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)\) (\(r\) summands).
383	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all \(r\le g\), the morphism
384	  %  \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r\) is birational; in particular,
385	  %  \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational map from \(C^{(g)}\) onto \(J\)."
386	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
387	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
388	
389	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
390	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
391	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
392	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
393	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold
394	  group-law addition \(\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J\) descends through
395	  \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to a regular morphism
396	  \[
397	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
398	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
399	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
400	  \]
401	  The morphism \(f^{(g)}\) is birational: it is dominant (its image is
402	  \(J\), the \(g\)-fold sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) exhausts
403	  \(J\) since \(\dim J = g\) and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
404	  its generic fibre is a single \(\bar k\)-point (a degree-\(g\) effective
405	  divisor \(D\) on \(C\) with \(h^0(D) = 1\) is the unique element of its linear
406	  system \(|D|\); such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
407	  C\( -- a consequence of \)\dim |D| = 0\( generically on a curve of genus \)g$
408	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational morphism in
409	  the sense that there exist dense opens
410	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\), \(V \subseteq J\) such that
411	  \(f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V\) is an isomorphism.
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	% NOTE (iter-199): the birationality proof below derives the generic-fibre
415	% identification from Riemann--Roch at \(\deg D = g\) (specifically
416	% \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = 1\) generically). Riemann--Roch is the Route C
417	% chain (\texttt{RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex} et al.), currently PAUSED
418	% per USER 2026-05-28 standing directive. This lemma is therefore
419	% gated on Route C re-engagement (or on a parallel construction that
420	% sidesteps RR). Flagged here so a future prover does not attempt to
421	% formalize the birationality argument until the RR substrate is
422	% available.
423	
424	\begin{proof}
425	
426	  Symmetry of \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}\) is the
427	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
428	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
429	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
430	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
431	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
432	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
433	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
434	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
435	
436	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
437	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
438	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
439	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
440	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
441	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
442	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
443	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
444	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
445	
446	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
447	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
448	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =
449	  0\( (the locus where \)h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
450	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
451	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
452	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
453	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).
454	
455	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the
456	  Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
457	  reading of \(f^{(g)}\) (effective divisor class \(\mapsto\) degree-zero
458	  line bundle class via \(D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]\)) is the
459	  same as the Yoneda image of the \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)-point
460	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
461	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]\(, where \)\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
462	  C\( is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree \)g\( on \)C$. We
463	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
464	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism \(f^{(g)}\) and its
465	  birationality.
466	\end{proof}
467	
468	\section{Descent through the birational quotient \(\sigma\)}
469	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
470	
471	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
472	morphism on \(C^g\) (after passing through \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) via
473	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting \(f^{(g)}\) via
474	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
475	\(J\). The output is a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), which is then
476	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
477	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
478	proof that consumes A.4.c.
479	
480	\begin{lemma}\leanok
481	[Descent of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\)]
482	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
483	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
484	  
485	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
486	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
487	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
488	  % "It therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
489	  %  shows to be a regular map."
490	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
491	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
492	  map'').}
493	
494	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
495	  over \(\bar k\). Let \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)
496	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
497	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) the birational morphism of
498	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
499	  regular morphism
500	  \[
501	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
502	  \]
503	  such that \(\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) on the dense open
504	  on which \(f^{(g)}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(\psi\) is the unique
505	  regular morphism extending the rational map
506	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
507	  A$).
508	\end{lemma}
509	
510	\begin{proof}
511	  
512	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
513	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and \(V \subseteq J\) with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
514	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\( an isomorphism. Setting \)\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
515	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
516	  \colon V \to U \to A\( produces a rational map \)J \dashrightarrow A$.
517	
518	  The target \(A\) is an abelian variety and the source \(J\) is smooth over
519	  \(\bar k\) (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
520	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
521	  \(\psi_0\) to a unique regular morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) extending
522	  \(\psi_0\) on \(V\). Uniqueness of \(\psi\) follows from the
523	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
524	  \(J \to A\) agreeing on a dense open are equal.
525	\end{proof}
526	
527	\section{Proof of the universal property}
528	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
529	
530	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
531	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
532	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
533	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
534	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
535	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
536	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
537	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
538	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
539	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
540	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
541	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
542	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
543	  
544	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
545	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
546	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
547	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
548	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
549	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
550	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
551	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
552	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
553	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
554	  \begin{enumerate}
555	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
556	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
557	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
558	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
559	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
560	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
561	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
562	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
563	      \[
564	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
565	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
566	      \]
567	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
568	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
569	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
570	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
571	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
572	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
573	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
574	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
575	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
576	      to a regular morphism
577	      \[
578	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
579	      \]
580	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
581	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
582	      \[
583	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
584	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
585	      \]
586	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
587	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
588	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
589	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
590	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
591	      \(\bar k\)-points,
592	      \[
593	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
594	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
595	      \]
596	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
597	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
598	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
599	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
600	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
601	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
602	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
603	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
604	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
605	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
606	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
607	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
608	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
609	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
610	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
611	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
612	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
613	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
614	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
615	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
616	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
617	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
618	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
619	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
620	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
621	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
622	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
623	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
624	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
625	  \end{enumerate}
626	  This completes the proof.
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
630	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
631	
632	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
633	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
634	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
635	fields are supplied by A.3:
636	\begin{itemize}
637	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
638	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
639	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
640	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
641	    refinement).
642	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
643	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
644	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
645	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
646	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
647	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
648	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
649	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
650	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
651	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
652	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
653	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
654	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
655	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
656	\end{itemize}
657	
658	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
659	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
660	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
661	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
662	
663	\section{Lean encoding}
664	\label{sec:albaneseup_lean_encoding}
665	
666	The Lean target file is the new
667	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean} (declared at the top of
668	this chapter). The five target declarations are:
669	\begin{itemize}
670	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}: the regular morphism
671	    \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}.
672	    The signature exposes the marked point \(P_0\) as an explicit argument and
673	    the Picard-scheme construction \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an existing object.
674	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}: the scheme-theoretic
675	    \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
676	    packaged with the canonical projection
677	    \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and its universal property
678	    (factorisation of every \(S_g\)-symmetric morphism out of \(C^g\)). \emph{This
679	    declaration has no Mathlib analogue}: Mathlib's \texttt{Sym} /
680	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} cover types and modules only; the
681	    scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied project-side, following
682	    Milne's affine-and-glue recipe of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}.
683	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}: the
684	    symmetrised morphism
685	    \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\) produced by
686	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} from an input morphism
687	    \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) into an abelian variety.
688	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}: the
689	    birational morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) of
690	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, together with the data of a
691	    dense open isomorphism \(U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\) realising
692	    birationality. The signature exposes the basepoint \(P_0\) and consumes
693	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}.
694	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}: the
695	    descent lemma of \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma},
696	    consuming \texttt{symmetricPowerAVMap} and
697	    \texttt{symmetricPowerToJacobian} together with
698	    \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
699	    (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to produce the regular
700	    morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\).
701	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese\_universal\_property}: the
702	    main theorem, the existence-and-uniqueness statement of
703	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}. The signature is
704	    \[
705	      \texttt{P\_0 : C(\bar k)} \;\to\;
706	      \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
707	      (\varphi : C \to A) \;\to\;

735	\section{Out of scope}
736	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
737	
738	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
739	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
740	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
741	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
742	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
743	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
744	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
745	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
746	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
747	explicitly out of scope:
748	\begin{itemize}
749	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (A.2.c).} Proved in
750	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
751	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
752	  \item \textbf{The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and the degree map
753	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
754	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
755	    consumes \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
756	    \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\) but does not construct it.
757	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
758	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
759	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
760	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
761	    rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism.
762	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\).}
763	    Mathlib has no \(g\)-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
764	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
765	    project-side sub-build (affine \(S_g\)-invariants
766	    \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\) on each open affine \(\Spec A \subseteq C\),
767	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
768	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
769	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
770	    target but does not develop its construction.
771	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\) (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
772	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
773	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of \(A\) and the
774	    autoduality of \(J\), which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
775	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
776	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route

778	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
779	    of this file.
780	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
781	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for \(C \times C \to A\),
782	    the identification of \((J, F)\) with the Albanese variety in the sense
783	    of Lang, and the autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\)). Not needed by the
784	    symmetric-power route.
785	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of \(J\) from the rational law of
786	    composition on \(C^{(g)}\) (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
787	    this alternative construction.
788	  \item \textbf{The genus-\(0\) case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
789	    is \(g(C) > 0\) (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-\(0\)
790	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by
791	    \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}), routed through
792	    \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} and the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
793	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the
794	    Pic\(^0\)-route of this chapter.
795	  \item \textbf{Generalisations to a non-algebraically-closed base \(k\).}
796	    The chapter works over \(\bar k\) throughout. The descent of the witness
797	    back to \(k\) via Galois descent of morphism equality is the subject of
798	    \cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f.
799	\end{itemize}

18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,

29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)

227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
232	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
233	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and

239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
242	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
243	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
244	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial at b80f227 carries a
245	% zero AddMonoidHom body (\(0 : \dots \to \dots\)), i.e.\ the zero map
246	% between two trivial-group endpoints, NOT the pullback-descended
247	% homomorphism this lemma describes. Moreover, because the codomain's
248	% \texttt{Zero} instance is derived from the sorry-body
249	% \texttt{addCommGroup} (the file-local genuine sorry of the chapter),
250	% the closure also inherits \texttt{sorryAx} via that typeclass leak;
251	% it is therefore not even axiom-clean. The closure is gated on the
252	% upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO
253	% NOT promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
254	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
255	% review, until the body is replaced with the mathematically correct
256	% map descended from \(g_C^*\).
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
266	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
267	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
339

559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}
597

702	The residual prover gate inside

705	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier --- that typed sorry was closed
